title: The CIA's Last Code
subject_line: For 35 years, no one could crack K4. Then a journalist made a phone call.
preheader: Not cryptanalysis, not a supercomputer — a footnote in an auction catalogue, and scraps of paper the artist forgot he'd given away.
disclaimer: Every Still at Nine story is built from documented sources. We tell you what was recorded, what was tested, and where the record runs out — and we leave the open questions open.

content_blocks.text: On September 3, 2025, an email arrived in the inbox of Jim Sanborn, the artist behind the most famous unsolved cipher. It contained the full decoded text of K4 — ninety-seven characters that professional cryptographers, NSA analysts, and AI systems had failed to solve for thirty-five years. The sender was not from an intelligence agency. He was a journalist in Los Angeles who had read a footnote in an auction catalogue. His method was not cryptanalysis. It was a phone call to an archivist.

It begins in a courtyard at CIA headquarters in Langley, Virginia, where a curved copper sculpture has stood since 1990. Sanborn designed it with Ed Scheidt, then chairman of the CIA's Cryptographic Center. They called it Kryptos. Stamped into the metal are nearly two thousand letters encoding four separate encrypted messages. The first three — K1, K2, and K3 — were solved during the 1990s. A CIA analyst cracked them by hand. An NSA team had quietly done it even earlier. A computer scientist announced his own solution publicly in 1999.

But K4, ninety-seven characters beginning with the letters OBKR, resisted everything. For thirty-five years, it drew sustained attention from cryptographers, hobbyists, intelligence professionals, and eventually machine-learning systems. None of them succeeded. Sanborn received so many claimed solutions over the years that he began charging fifty dollars per submission. Eventually he built an AI answering system just to handle the volume.

By 2025, Sanborn was seventy-nine and had endured a series of health scares. He announced plans to auction the solution to K4 through RR Auction in Boston, expected to fetch between three hundred thousand and half a million dollars. The auction catalogue described original coding charts held at the Smithsonian Institution's Archives of American Art.
That catalogue entry was the side door.

Jarett Kobek, a journalist and writer in Los Angeles, had developed a deep interest in Kryptos after the death of his father in 2023. He noticed the mention of the Smithsonian papers. He contacted his friend Richard Byrne, a journalist and experienced archival researcher based in Washington, D.C. Byrne requested access to Sanborn's donated papers at the Smithsonian. Inside a folder, they found taped-together scraps of paper containing K4's original plaintext — identifiable because the scraps included phrases matching clues Sanborn had previously released, among them "BERLIN CLOCK" and "EAST NORTHEAST."

On September 3, Kobek and Byrne emailed the decoded text to Sanborn. He confirmed its authenticity. The explanation was disarmingly simple: roughly a decade earlier, while compiling papers during cancer treatment, Sanborn had mistakenly gathered the scraps among the documents he donated to the Smithsonian. They had sat there unnoticed ever since.

Sanborn asked the Smithsonian to seal the relevant archives. The institution complied, blocking access to the materials until 2075. RR Auction threatened legal action against the journalists. Both Kobek and Byrne told the New York Times they had no intention of releasing the plaintext publicly.

In November 2025, Sanborn appeared at the International Spy Museum in Washington. His message was firm: K4 had not been solved. What the journalists had found was the scrambled plaintext — the answer — but not the method of encryption. The cryptographic puzzle, as a puzzle, remained open.

In an open letter to the Kryptos community, he released four new clues. The solution references a 1986 trip he took to Egypt and the fall of the Berlin Wall. The previously released "BERLINCLOCK" clue refers to the World Clock in Berlin. The codes are about delivering a message. And a fifth coded message — K5, also ninety-seven characters long — exists, thematically connected to the text of K2, which reads, in part, "it's buried out there somewhere." K5 would be placed in a public space when K4 is properly solved.

On November 20, 2025, the full Kryptos archive sold at auction for nearly a million dollars to an anonymous bidder. The buyer would receive a private meeting with Sanborn, the coding charts, and the artistic intent behind K4 and K5. A stewardship plan for the materials was still being developed.

content_blocks.label: The detail that does not fit

content_blocks.text: In the language of modern cryptography, what Kobek and Byrne performed was a side-channel attack — a term for extracting a secret not by breaking the code itself but by finding a vulnerability in how the secret was stored or transmitted. Scientific American noted that their discovery mirrors real-world security breaches: the front door holds, but someone walks through the side entrance.

The answer was not computed, decoded, or brute-forced. It was found by a journalist who read an auction catalogue, called an archivist, and recognised scraps of paper the artist himself did not know he had given away.

Sanborn has also acknowledged that he was not entirely truthful with Scheidt about the full solution — meaning even the co-designer may not know the complete picture. As Sanborn told the Associated Press: the plaintext was discovered, not deciphered.

The sculpture still stands in the courtyard at Langley. Ninety-seven characters, beginning with OBKR, stamped into copper. Two journalists know what they say. An anonymous buyer now holds the archive. The method — the actual cipher — remains unsolved. And somewhere, according to Sanborn, a fifth message is waiting — buried out there somewhere.

sources.text: Scientific American — A Solution to the CIA's Kryptos Code Is Found after 35 Years (Oct 2025)
Scientific American — CIA Kryptos Puzzle Creator Releases Final Clues (Nov 2025)
Scientific American — How the CIA's Kryptos Sculpture Gave Up Its Final Secret (Oct 2025)
WBUR / NPR — Info to Decipher CIA Sculpture Message Sells for Close to $1M at Boston Auction (Nov 2025)
